elements.sent-2411-001: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have shown promising results in modeling graphs in various tasks.
elements.sent-2411-002: The training of GNNs, especially on specialized tasks such as bioinformatics, demands extensive expert annotations, which are expensive and usually contain sensitive information of data providers.
elements.sent-2411-003: The trained GNN models are often shared for deployment in the real world.
elements.sent-2411-004: As neural networks can memorize the training samples, the model parameters of GNNs have a high risk of leaking private training data.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our theoretical analysis shows the strong connections between trained GNN parameters and the training graphs used, confirming the training graph leakage issue.
elements.sent-2411-006: However, explorations into training data leakage from trained GNNs are rather limited.
elements.sent-2411-007: Therefore, we investigate a novel problem of stealing graphs from trained GNNs.
elements.sent-2411-008: To obtain high-quality graphs that resemble the target training set, a graph diffusion model with diffusion noise optimization is deployed as a graph generator.
elements.sent-2411-009: Furthermore, we propose a selection method that effectively leverages GNN model parameters to identify training graphs from samples generated by the graph diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-010: Extensive experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework in stealing training graphs from the trained GNN.
elements.sent-2411-011: Graph-structured data pervade numerous real-world applications such as social networks~\cite{hamilton2017inductive , finance systems~\cite{wang2019semi , and molecular graphs~\cite{wang2022molecular .}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have shown promising results in modeling graphs by adopting a message passing scheme~\cite{kipf2016semi,xu2018powerful,wang2024efficient , which updates a node's representation by aggregating information from its neighbors.}
elements.sent-2411-013: The learned representation can preserve both node attributes and local graph structural information, facilitating various tasks, such as node classification~\cite{ and graph classifications~\cite{errica2019fair .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: As shown in Fig~\ref{fig:illustration_of_GNN_training_releasing , generally, abundant training data is required to train a high-performance GNN model.}
elements.sent-2411-015: This becomes particularly expensive for critical applications such as molecule property prediction, which demands expert annotations.
elements.sent-2411-016: Moreover, the training data may hold sensitive information belonging to its providers.
elements.sent-2411-017: Consequently, protecting the privacy of the training data is imperative.
elements.sent-2411-018: After training on the private training data, the trained GNN model is often released for downstream applications.
elements.sent-2411-019: For example, a well-trained molecule property predictor may be made open source, supporting the direct deployment for the designed task or model initialization for other tasks.
elements.sent-2411-020: However, neural networks can memorize the training data, even when they have great generalization ability~\cite{zhang2021understanding .}
elements.sent-2411-021: It is also demonstrated that the trained parameters of MLP are linear combinations of the derivatives of the network at a set of training data points~\cite{Lyu2020Gradient , causing potential private data leakage.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Our theoretical analysis in Theorem~\ref{thm:relationship_parameter_training_data further shows that the above observations can be extended to GNNs.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Hence, releasing GNN models potentially threatens the privacy of the private training data.
elements.sent-2411-024: Therefore, in this paper, we investigate a novel problem of stealing training graphs from trained GNN models when the model architecture and parameters are known/released.
elements.sent-2411-025: Several initial efforts are made in model inversion attacks, which aim to reconstruct graph topologies~\cite{zhang2022inference or infer the sensitive node attributes attributes~\cite{duddu2020quantifying .}}
elements.sent-2411-026: However, they are proposed to reconstruct the train graph/node from node/graph embeddings, which cannot be applied for training graph stealing as embeddings of private training data are unavailable for attackers.
elements.sent-2411-027: Recently, GraphMI~\cite{zhang2021graphmi proposes to reconstruct the adjacency matrix of the training graph in a white-box setting where the trained model parameters are available.}
elements.sent-2411-028: However, GraphMI requires all the node attributes of the training graph to be available for topology reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-029: This assumption is impractical in real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-030: Therefore, in this paper, we propose to steal the training graphs from the trained GNN model without any information of the training data, which is not explored by existing works.
elements.sent-2411-031: However, it is non-trivial to perform the graph stealing attack.
elements.sent-2411-032: There are two major challenges to be addressed.
elements.sent-2411-033: First, it is challenging to ensure both the realism and quality of graphs reconstructed from the trained GNN model.
elements.sent-2411-034: Existing model inversion attacks on GNNs mainly focus at the node level, concentrating on reconstructing either links~\cite{he2021stealing,zhang2021graphmi or node attributes~\cite{duddu2020quantifying,zhang2022inference of the target graphs.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: However, this focus is inadequate for graph stealing attacks because graph-structured data comprises both graph topology and node attributes, rendering these methods insufficient for comprehensive graph-level reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-036: Our empirical analysis in Sec.~\ref{sec:comparisions_with_baselines further demonstrates that current methods encounter difficulties in providing realistic and high-quality graphs during graph-level reconstruction.}
elements.sent-2411-037: Second, how to effectively utilize the trained GNN model parameters to extract the training graph information? Previous works on model inversion attacks on GNNs~\cite{duddu2020quantifying,he2021stealing,zhang2021graphmi typically rely on comprehensive side information of target samples for graph reconstruction.}
elements.sent-2411-038: The investigations on extracting training data information from trained GNN parameters without any partial training data information poses a new challenge.
elements.sent-2411-039: In an effort to address the aforementioned challenges, we propose a novel Graph Stealing attack framework (GraphSteal).
elements.sent-2411-040: Specifically, to overcome the challenge of reconstructing high-quality graphs, we employ a graph diffusion model as the graph generator.
elements.sent-2411-041: In addition, this graph diffusion model used in GraphSteal adopts a diffusion noise optimization algorithm, which can produce a set of candidate graphs that more closely resemble the training set of the target GNN.
elements.sent-2411-042: Moreover, according to our theoretical analysis in Theorem~\ref{thm:relationship_parameter_training_data , there is a strong connection between the GNN model parameters and training data.}
elements.sent-2411-043: Based on the theorem, we propose a model parameter-guided graph selection method, which leverages the parameters of GNNs to identify the training graphs from the candidate graph set generated by the graph diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-044: In summary, our main contributions are:.
elements.sent-2411-045: We study a new problem of stealing training graphs from a trained GNN without any partial information on training data;.
elements.sent-2411-046: We propose GraphSteal, a novel attack framework that can effectively recreate high-quality graphs that are part of the target training dataset by leveraging parameters of GNN models; and.
elements.sent-2411-047: Extensive experiments on various real-world datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed GraphSteal in accurately reconstructing private training graphs of trained GNN models.

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Reconstruction Algorithm of GraphSteal} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE Auxiliary dataset $\mathcal{D}_a$, trained target GNN classifier $f_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$ with parameters $\boldsymbol{\theta}$, hyperparameter $\alpha$ and the selection number $k$ \ENSURE Reconstructed graphs set $\mathcal{D}_{r}$ \STATE Train a graph generator $h_{\mathcal{G}}$ on $\mathcal{D}_a$ \STATE Select input graphs from $\mathcal{D}_a$ based on Eq.~(\ref{eq:metric_score_for_selecting_input_graphs}) and obtain $\mathcal{D}_c$ \STATE Optimize $\mathcal{D}_c$ to get $\mathcal{D}'_c$ based on Eq.~(\ref{eq:noise_optimization_objective_function}) \STATE Generate graphs $\mathcal{D}_{g}$ based on Eq.~(\ref{eq:sdedit_geneartion}) \WHILE{not converged} \STATE Calculate $\mathcal{L}_{s}(\mathcal{D}_{g}, \Lambda, \boldsymbol{\theta})$ based on Eq.~(\ref{eq:selection_mask_objective}) \STATE Calculate $\mathcal{L}_{\lambda}(\Lambda)$ based on Eq.~(\ref{eq:contraint_loss}). \STATE Update the selection masks $\Lambda$ with gradient decent on $\nabla_{\Lambda}(\mathcal{L}_{s}+\alpha\mathcal{L}_{\lambda})$ based on Eq.~(\ref{eq:final_objective_function}). \ENDWHILE \STATE Select graphs with the top-$k$ highest value in $\Lambda$ as $\mathcal{D}_r$ \label{alg:reconstructioin_alg} \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-173: In this section, we conduct experiments on various real-world datasets to answer the following research questions: (i) Q1: Can GraphSteal effectively reconstruct private training graphs of GNNs by leveraging the parameters of GNNs? (ii) Q2: How do the number of final selected graphs affect the performance of reconstruction? (iii) Q3: How does each component of GraphSteal contribute to the effectiveness in stealing training graphs?.
elements.sent-2411-174: We conduct experiments on 3 public real-world datasets, i.e., FreeSolv, ESOL and QM9~\cite{wu2018moleculenet .}
elements.sent-2411-175: FreeSolv and ESOL are small-scale molecular datasets.
elements.sent-2411-177: For each experiment, $20\%$ of randomly selected graphs from the original dataset serves as the target dataset for training the target GNN classifier.
elements.sent-2411-178: Additionally, another $10\%$ of the graphs are set as the validation set, while the remaining $70\%$ are assigned as the test set.
elements.sent-2411-179: Note that we set the test set as the auxiliary datase for attackers to train the graph generator and conduct reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-180: The statistics of the datasets are summarized in Tab.~\ref{tab:dataset_statistics .}
elements.sent-2411-181: More details of the dataset settings are shown in Appendix~\ref{appendix:datasets .}
elements.sent-2411-182: To the best of our knowledge, GraphSteal is the first graph stealing attack to extract training graphs without access to the private graph-level dataset.
elements.sent-2411-183: To demonstrate the effectiveness of GraphSteal, we first propose three variants:.
elements.sent-2411-184: BL-Rand: It randomly generates graphs using Erdos-Renyi (ER) models, where node features are randomly sampled from the graphs in the auxiliary dataset.
elements.sent-2411-185: BL-Conf: This method directly selects the top-$k$ most confidence graphs from the auxiliary dataset as the reconstructed graphs.
elements.sent-2411-186: BL-Diff: It applies the graph diffusion model trained on the auxiliary datasets to generate graphs without any modification and selection.
elements.sent-2411-187: Moreover, we compare GraphSteal with a state-of-the-art model inversion attack method for graph neural networks:.
elements.sent-2411-188: GraphMI-G: This method is extended from GraphMI~\cite{zhang2021graphmi , which is designed for node classification.}
elements.sent-2411-189: Specifically, GraphMI aims to reconstruct the adjacency matrix of the target graphs based on the node features and labels of the target graphs.
elements.sent-2411-190: To adapt it to our setting, where there is no partial information about the target dataset, we randomly select graphs from the auxiliary dataset as input graphs.
elements.sent-2411-191: We then apply GraphMI to modify the adjacency matrices of these graphs.
elements.sent-2411-192: The modified graphs are finally regarded as the reconstructed graphs.
elements.sent-2411-193: Additionally, we also consider a state-of-the-art model-level explanation method as the baseline:.
elements.sent-2411-194: XGNN~\cite{yuan2020xgnn : It generates representative graphs for each class that the target classifier is most confident with as model-level explanations.}
elements.sent-2411-195: To adapt it to our setting, we treat these generated representative graphs as reconstructed training graphs.

elements.sent-2411-201: First, we adopt the following metrics to evaluate the realism and quality of the reconstructed graphs:.
elements.sent-2411-202: Validity calculates the fraction of the reconstructed molecular graphs adhering to fundamental chemical rules and principles as $\text{Validity = V(\mathcal{D _r)/|\mathcal{D _r|$, where $V(\mathcal{D _r)$ is the number of graphs in $\mathcal{D _r$ adhering to fundamental chemical rules and principles measured by RDKit~\footnote{https://www.rdkit.org/ .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-203: A larger validity score implies the better quality and realism of the reconstructed graphs.
elements.sent-2411-204: Uniqueness measures the proportion of graphs that exhibit uniqueness within the entire set of reconstructed graphs, which is calculated by $\text{Uniqueness = U(\mathcal{D _r) / |\mathcal{D _r|$, where $U(\mathcal{D _r)$ is the number of unique graphs within $\mathcal{D _r$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-205: A larger uniqueness score implies a better diversity in reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-206: Second, we apply the following metrics to evaluate the fidelity of the reconstructed graphs in mirroring the graphs in the target dataset:.
elements.sent-2411-207: Reconstruction Rate represents the fraction of reconstructed graphs matching those in the target dataset as Reconstruction rate $= |\mathcal{D _r \cap \mathcal{D _t|/|\mathcal{D _r|$, where $|\mathcal{D _r \cap \mathcal{D _t|$ denotes the number of reconstructed graphs exactly matched with those in $\mathcal{D _t$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-208: A larger reconstruction rate indicates a better reconstruction method.

elements.sent-2411-262: In this subsection, we conduct a case study to further demonstrate the effectiveness of GraphSteal.
elements.sent-2411-263: We conduct graph stealing attack using both GraphMI-G and GraphSteal on QM9 and then visualize the top-$8$ representative reconstructed graphs.
elements.sent-2411-264: The visualizations are plotted in Fig.~\ref{fig:visualization .}
elements.sent-2411-265: From the figure, we observe that the graphs reconstructed by GraphMI-G appear unrealistic and invalid, which implies the poor performance of GraphMI-G in graph stealing attacks.
elements.sent-2411-266: In contrast, the graphs reconstructed by our method exhibit both high quality and realism, thereby verifying the effectiveness of our method in reconstructing high-quality graphs by leveraging the model parameters of GNNs.
elements.sent-2411-267: In this paper, we study a novel privacy attack problem of stealing training graphs from trained GNNs.
elements.sent-2411-268: We propose a novel framework, GraphSteal, to reconstruct training graphs by leveraging the parameters of GNNs.
elements.sent-2411-269: Specifically, we apply a graph diffusion model as the graph generator to reconstruct graphs realistically.
elements.sent-2411-270: We then implement a diffusion noise optimization to enhance the resemblance of the graphs generated by the graph generator to the target training data.
elements.sent-2411-271: Moreover, a model parameter-guided graph selection method is proposed to identify the training graphs from the generated graphs by leveraging the parameters of GNNs.
elements.sent-2411-272: Extensive experiments on different real-world datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of GraphSteal in reconstructing realistic and high-quality training graphs from trained GNNs.
elements.sent-2411-273: There are two directions that need further investigation.
elements.sent-2411-274: First, in this paper, we only focus on performing graph stealing attack in the white-box setting.
elements.sent-2411-275: Thus, it is also interesting to in investigate how to conduct the black-box graph stealing attack effectively.
elements.sent-2411-276: Second, it is also worthwhile to investigate how to defend against the graph stealing attack.
elements.sent-2411-277: The discussions of the potential countermeasures and ethical implications are in Appendix~\ref{sec:countermeasures and Appendix~\ref{sec:ethical_implications , respectively.. \bibliographystyle{ACM-Reference-Format .}}}

elements.sent-2411-316: Given our focus on graph classification, we adapt these datasets to our setting by categorizing each graph according to the magnitude of its regression values and ensuring an equal division of graphs within each class.
elements.sent-2411-317: More specifically, for FreeSolv and ESOL, we partition the datasets into two classes, maintaining an equal number of graphs in each.
elements.sent-2411-318: To further validate the performance of GraphSteal in the multi-class graph classification task, we divide the QM9 dataset into three classes, also ensuring each class contains an equal number of graphs.
elements.sent-2411-319: FCD~\cite{preuer2018fcd is a popular metric used to calculate the distance between the distribution $p_{w (\cdot)$ of real-world molecules and the distribution $p(\cdot)$ of molecules from a generative model.}}
elements.sent-2411-320: To calculate this distance, firstly, we use the activations of the penultimate layer of ChemNet~\cite{goli2020chemnet to obtain the representation of each molecule.}
elements.sent-2411-321: We then calculate the mean and covariance of these activations for the two distributions.
elements.sent-2411-322: The two distributions $(p(\cdot), p_{w (\cdot))$ are compared using the Wasserstein-2 distance~\cite{vaserstein1969markov .}}
elements.sent-2411-323: Formally, FCD $d(\cdot,\cdot)$ is given by: \begin{equation}d^2((\mathbf{m},\mathbf{C}),(\mathbf{m}_w,\mathbf{C}_w)) = ||\mathbf{m}-\mathbf{m}_{w}||^2_2 + \text{Tr}(\mathbf{C}+\mathbf{C}_w-2\cdot(\mathbf{C}\mathbf{C}_w)^{1/2}),\end{equation} where $(\mathbf{m _w,\mathbf{C _w)$ are the mean and covariance of Gaussian $p_w(\cdot)$ obtained from real-world, and $(\mathbf{m ,\mathbf{C )$ are the mean and covariance of Gaussian $p(\cdot)$ obtained from the generative model.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-324: Note that FCD here is reported as $d^2(\cdot,\cdot)$ analogously to~\cite{heusel2017gans .}
elements.subsubsection-2411-017: How To Evaluate the Exactly Graph Matching?
elements.sent-2411-325: We utilize SMILES strings of molecule graphs for graph matching.
elements.sent-2411-326: SMILES (Simplified Molecular Input Line Entry System)~\cite{weininger1988smiles is a widely used notation in chem-informatics for encoding the structure of chemical molecules using short ASCII strings, thereby facilitating efficient representation and manipulation of chemical structures.}
elements.sent-2411-327: The reconstructed graph is matched with the target graph if their SMILES strings are identical~\cite{landrum2013rdkit, o2016comparing .}
elements.sent-2411-328: Specifically, let $\mathcal{T _r = \{\tau_1,\ldots,\tau_m\}$ and $\mathcal{T _t = \{\tau_1,\ldots,\tau_m\}$ denote the SMILES strings sets of reconstructed graphs $\mathcal{D _r$ and training graphs $\mathcal{D _t$, respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-329: Then, $\text{Reconstruction rate = |\mathcal{T _r\cap\mathcal{T _t|/|\mathcal{T _r|$, where $|\mathcal{T _r\cap\mathcal{T _t|$ denotes the number of the SMILES strings of reconstructed graphs exactly matched with those of graphs in $\mathcal{D _t$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-330: This method is efficient and reasonable for matching molecule graphs as SMILES strings cover various aspects of molecular structure, encoding information about atoms, bonds, and connectivity within a molecule.

elements.sent-2411-355: In this section, we explore the potential countermeasures of graph stealing attacks.
elements.sent-2411-356: Since graph stealing attacks represent a new type of privacy attack that steals private training graphs from trained GNN classifiers, there is no existing work studying the defense against this attack.
elements.sent-2411-357: One potential defense could be differential privacy (DP).
elements.sent-2411-358: Here, we investigate the effectiveness of DP against graph stealing attacks.
elements.sent-2411-359: Following \cite{abadi2016deep , we add Gaussian noise to the gradients in each training iteration of target GNN classifier training to ensure $(\epsilon,\delta)-\text{DP}$.}
elements.sent-2411-360: We fix $\delta = 10^{-5}$ and vary the noise scale to $\{1.0,5.0,10.0\}$.
elements.sent-2411-361: The reconstruction results on QM9 are reported in Tab.~\ref{tab:countermeasure .}
elements.sent-2411-362: From the table, we can observe that as the privacy budget $\epsilon$ decreases, GraphSteal consistently shows good validity, uniqueness, and FCD but only a slight drop in the reconstruction rate.
elements.sent-2411-363: This indicates that applying differential privacy to GNNs cannot prevent graph stealing attacks effectively.
elements.sent-2411-364: Therefore, there is an emerging need to design more effective countermeasures against GraphSteal.
elements.sent-2411-365: In this paper, we study a novel privacy attack problem of extracting private training graphs from the trained GNN.
elements.sent-2411-366: Our work uncovers the vulnerability of GNNs to the graph stealing attack and discusses potential countermeasures against this attack.
elements.sent-2411-367: Our work aims to raise awareness about the privacy issues inherent in GNNs and inspire the following works to develop more advanced privacy-preserving methods to protect against graph stealing attacks.
